text.title: Stakeholder Sentiment Tracker — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for an opt-in, weekly, three-class sentiment trend with an alarm trigger and a mandatory action-plan attachment.

rule.statement: Ingestion of any source artefact for sentiment tracking MUST be preceded by documented consent: either an organisation-level policy citation OR a per-stakeholder opt-in record stored with the consent date and the artefact scope. The PM MUST be able to produce the consent record on demand.

rationale.source: GDPR Art.6 lawful-basis patterns; corporate sentiment-monitoring case law

rule.rationale: Sentiment tracking on private communications without consent is a legal and trust-destroying risk. Storing the consent record as a precondition makes the entire pipeline defensible if it is ever questioned.

rule.statement: The classification rubric MUST use exactly three classes — `supportive`, `cautious`, `hostile` — with at least 5 explicit linguistic markers per class checked into the rubric file. Free-form sentiment scores (-1 to 1, 0-100) are not accepted because they hide the markers behind a number.

rationale.source: Reichheld NPS critique; closed-vocab sentiment classifier literature

rule.rationale: Three-class output is what PMs can act on; numeric scores are noise that invites cherry-picking the threshold. Pinned linguistic markers also make the classifier auditable when a stakeholder disputes a "hostile" label.

rule.statement: The pipeline MUST run on a fixed weekly cadence, at the same weekday/time, and MUST produce a per-stakeholder entry even when no new artefacts arrived (with an explicit "no signal" class). Skipping a week silently is forbidden.

rationale.source: Continuous-monitoring patterns (Allspaw); silence-as-signal in customer-success ops

rule.rationale: A "no signal" week is itself a data point — a stakeholder who has gone quiet is a known precursor to going hostile. Letting the pipeline skip silently loses that signal.

rule.statement: An alarm MUST fire when EITHER (a) a stakeholder's classification declines for 2 consecutive weeks (supportive→cautious or cautious→hostile), OR (b) any single week's classification is `hostile`. The alarm MUST be delivered to the PM channel within 24 hours of the run completing.
rule.rationale: Two-week decline catches the silent drift pattern; single-week hostile catches the acute event. Together they cover both failure modes without flooding the PM with noise on normal weeks.

rule.statement: An alarm MUST be paired with a written PM action plan within 72 hours, containing: (a) hypothesised cause, (b) named intervention (call / meeting / written response), (c) deadline. Alarms left without an action plan past 72 hours block the next sentiment run.

rationale.source: OKR retro discipline; PDCA loop closure

rule.rationale: An alarm without an action is a notification, not a tool. Blocking the next run is the only enforcement that survives a busy PM week; otherwise alarms accumulate and the system decays into noise.

example.code:
# Sentiment run — 2026-05-20
## Stakeholder: Maria Lopes (VP Ops, sponsor) - Sources: 14 emails, 3 meeting transcripts (consent: 2026-01-10) - Markers hit: "concerned", "let's reconsider", "timeline slipping" - Class: cautious (down from supportive last week — 2-week decline trend) - Trend (last 6 weeks): supportive, supportive, supportive, supportive, cautious, cautious
## ALARM - Stakeholder: Maria Lopes - Reason: 2-week decline (supportive → cautious → cautious) - Action plan (filed 2026-05-22): - Hypothesis: April milestone slip eroded confidence. - Intervention: 30-min 1:1 with Maria, walk through revised plan + slip RCA. - Deadline: 2026-05-24
example.note: Consent recorded. Three-class output. Trend visible. Alarm fired and paired with an action plan within 72h.

example.code:
# Sentiment dashboard
| Stakeholder | Score | |-------------|-------| | Maria Lopes | 4/10 | | Tomás Silva | 7/10 |
(No prior weeks shown; no consent record; no alarm threshold defined.)
example.note: Free-form score, no trend, no consent trail, no alarm policy, no action plan. Functionally a feeling expressed in numbers.
